verses.flow: The burden (massa) of the word of YHWH to Israel by the hand of Malachi.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "massa" (Strong's H4853) means a burden or oracle, often used for weighty prophetic pronouncements (cf. Isa 13:1; Nah 1:1). "Malachi" (H4401) likely means "my messenger," which may be a title rather than a personal name, anticipating the "messenger" theme in 3:1. The phrase "by the hand of" (be-yad) is a standard formula for prophetic agency.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 2nd masc plur
verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: I have loved you, says YHWH. But you say, How have you loved us? Was not Esau a brother to Jacob? declares YHWH. Yet I have loved Jacob,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's declaration "I have loved Jacob" establishes the covenantal basis of the entire book. The Hebrew verb ahav (H157) here carries covenantal election overtones, not merely sentiment. The people's response ("How have you loved us?") reveals a crisis of faith — they cannot perceive YHWH's covenant faithfulness (hesed) amid their present circumstances, likely the post-exilic disillusionment of the 5th century BC.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: but Esau I have hated; and I have made his mountains a desolation and his inheritance a waste for the jackals of the wilderness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between Jacob and Esau reflects the sovereign election of YHWH (cf. Rom 9:13, where Paul cites this verse to illustrate divine election). "Hate" (sane, H8130) in Semitic idiom often means "to love less" or "to set aside," though it also carries the force of judicial rejection. The desolation of Edom's mountains is a historical reality fulfilled after the Babylonian and subsequent Nabataean pressures on Edomite territory.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common plur
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common plur

words.g: to pull down or in pieces

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: If Edom says, We are shattered, but we will return and rebuild the ruins — thus says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot): They may build, but I will tear down; and they will be called the territory of wickedness and the people with whom YHWH is angry forever.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot)" — this title (H6635) refers to YHWH as commander of heavenly and earthly armies and appears 24 times in Malachi alone, its highest concentration in any prophetic book. Edom (likely the Edomites displaced into the Negev, becoming the Idumeans) attempted to resettle but YHWH declares their rebuilding futile. The phrase "territory of wickedness" (gebul rish'ah) assigns a spiritual identity to the land itself.

verses.flow: Your own eyes will see, and you yourselves will say, Great is YHWH beyond the border of Israel.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: A son honors his father, and a servant his master. If then I am a father, where is my honor? And if I am a master, where is my fear? says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot) to you, O priests who despise my name. But you say, How have we despised your name?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The father-son and master-servant metaphors establish the covenantal relational framework YHWH uses to indict the priests. The priests bear the special obligation of honoring YHWH's name, yet they are the primary violators. "Fear" (yir'ah, H3374) in this context denotes reverential awe owed to a sovereign, not mere anxiety. This verse introduces the priestly controversy that dominates chapters 1-2.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
verb piel perfect 1st common plur + suffix
preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: By offering upon my altar polluted bread (lechem). But you say, How have we polluted you? By saying the table of YHWH is despicable.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Polluted bread" (lechem megoa'al) — the root ga'al (H1351) means to defile or contaminate through ritual uncleanness. The "table of YHWH" is the altar of sacrifice, described here with contempt by the priests. This echoes the Mosaic legislation of Lev 22:17-25, which explicitly forbids blemished offerings. The priests have redefined acceptable worship on their own terms, a timeless corruption of liturgical practice.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc plur + suffix
verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: When you offer the blind as sacrifice, is that not evil? And when you offer the lame and the sick, is that not evil? Present it now to your governor — will he be pleased with you, or will he lift up your face? says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing + suffix
particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And now, entreat the face of God that he may be gracious (chanan) to us. With such an offering from your hand, will he lift up any face? says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Oh that there were even one among you who would shut the doors, so that you would not kindle fire on my altar in vain! I have no pleasure in you, says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot), and an offering from your hand I will not accept.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's wish that someone would "shut the doors" of the temple is a startling statement — He would prefer the cessation of corrupt worship to its continuation. This anticipates prophetic critique seen in Amos 5:21-24 and Isa 1:11-15. The principle established here is that insincere or ritually defective worship is not neutral; it is an offense to YHWH. This verse carries profound implications for NT teaching on worship in spirit and truth (John 4:24).

verses.flow: For from the rising of the sun even to its setting, great is my name among the nations (goyim); and in every place incense is offered to my name, and a pure offering — for great is my name among the nations, says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is one of the most remarkable universalist prophecies in the OT, declaring that YHWH's name will be great "among the nations (goyim)" and that a "pure offering" will be made in every place. Early church fathers (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) understood this as a prophecy of the Eucharist and the universal spread of the gospel. Whether fulfilled through the global church's worship or eschatologically, it anticipates the Gentile inclusion central to the NT (cf. Mal 1:11 with Acts 15:14-17; Rev 5:9).

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: But you profane it when you say, The table of YHWH is polluted, and its fruit, its food, is despicable.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

particle + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And you say, What a weariness this is! And you sniff at it, says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot). You bring the stolen and the lame and the sick — and you bring the offering! Shall I accept that from your hand? says YHWH.

Cursed be the deceiver who has in his flock a male and vows it, yet sacrifices to my Lord (Adon) that which is blemished. For I am a great King, says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot), and my name is feared among the nations (goyim).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "Adon" (H113, my Lord/Master) used here alongside "YHWH of hosts" emphasizes YHWH's absolute sovereignty. The declaration "I am a great King" and "my name is feared among the nations" evokes the divine council imagery of Ps 47:2 and Ps 95:3, where YHWH is acclaimed as the great King over all elohim and all peoples. The concluding universal scope — "among the nations" — forms an inclusio with v.11 and frames all corrupt Israelite worship against the backdrop of YHWH's cosmic kingship.

verses.flow: And now, to you is this commandment, O priests.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: If you will not hear, and if you will not lay it to heart to give glory to my name, says YHWH of hosts, then I will send the curse upon you and I will curse your blessings; and indeed I have cursed them, because you are not laying it to heart.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "curse" here is 'arar (Strong's H779), distinct from the covenant curse term 'alah. The irony is sharp: the priestly blessing (Num 6:22-27) becomes the vehicle of curse when the priests themselves are faithless. THEOLOGICAL: The priest's vocation was to mediate blessing between YHWH and the people; their failure inverts the very purpose of their office, a pattern seen in corrupt leadership throughout the prophets.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Behold, I am rebuking your offspring (zera'), and I will spread dung upon your faces — the dung of your festivals — and you shall be carried away with it.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: And you shall know that I have sent this commandment to you, that my covenant might be with Levi, says YHWH of hosts.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: My covenant was with him — life and peace (shalom), and I gave them to him as an object of fear; and he feared me and was in awe before my name.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "covenant with Levi" is not explicitly recorded as a formal covenant elsewhere in the Torah, but is alluded to in Num 25:12-13 (Phinehas) and Deut 33:8-11. This covenant encompasses both life and shalom (peace/wholeness, Strong's H7965), indicating total well-being as its gift. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the ultimate High Priest fulfills and surpasses the Levitical ideal described here (Heb 7:11-28).

verses.flow: True instruction (torah) was in his mouth, and unrighteousness was not found on his lips; in peace (shalom) and in uprightness he walked with me, and he turned many from iniquity.
For the lips of a priest shall keep knowledge, and instruction (torah) shall men seek from his mouth; for he is the messenger of YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word for "messenger" is mal'ak (Strong's H4397), the same word used for angelic messengers and the title of the book of Malachi itself ("my messenger"). THEOLOGICAL: The priest as mal'ak establishes a mediatorial, ambassadorial role — he speaks the words of YHWH to the people. This anticipates the prophetic and priestly role of Christ, and the teaching office of the New Covenant community.

verses.flow: But you have turned aside from the way; you have caused many to stumble through the instruction (torah); you have corrupted the covenant of Levi, says YHWH of hosts.
And so I also have made you contemptible and lowly before all the people, according as you have not been keeping my ways but have been lifting up faces in the instruction (torah).

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Do we not all have one father? Did not one God create us? Why do we deal treacherously each man against his brother, profaning the covenant of our fathers?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The appeal to one Father and one Creator grounds the ethical demand in theology proper — covenant faithfulness among humans derives from the singular creative and paternal act of God. HISTORICAL: This verse has been debated as to whether "one father" refers to Abraham, Jacob, or God himself; the parallelism with "one God created us" strongly favors a theological rather than patriarchal referent.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Judah has dealt treacherously, and an abomination has been committed in Israel and in Jerusalem; for Judah has profaned the holy thing of YHWH, which he loves, and has married the daughter of a foreign god.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

words.g: and to be or come near

verses.flow: May YHWH cut off from the man who does this — the one who is wakeful and the one who answers — out of the tents of Jacob, even him who brings near an offering to YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "the one who is wakeful and the one who answers" ('er we'oneh) is notoriously difficult; it may be an idiom meaning "every last person," possibly referring to the one who calls and the one who responds in a legal or liturgical setting. The imprecatory form here calls down covenant sanctions upon the covenant-breaker.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: And this second thing you do: you cover the altar of YHWH with tears, with weeping and sighing, because he no longer turns toward the offering or accepts it with favor from your hand.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: And you say, "For what reason?" Because YHWH has been a witness between you and the wife of your youth, against whom you have dealt treacherously; she is your companion and the wife of your covenant.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH is named as a legal witness ('ed, Strong's H5707) to the marriage covenant, grounding the sanctity of marriage in the divine council and covenant framework. TYPOLOGY: The marriage covenant between a man and the wife of his youth serves as an earthly image of YHWH's covenant bond with Israel (Hos 2; Eph 5:25-32), making marital treachery a form of covenant apostasy.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: And did he not make them one? And a remnant of the spirit (ruach) belonged to him. And why one? He was seeking a godly offspring (zera'). Therefore guard yourselves in your spirit (ruach), and let none of you deal treacherously against the wife of his youth.
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse is among the most textually difficult in the Hebrew Bible. The phrase rendered "a remnant of the spirit (ruach)" likely means God retained the capacity to create more, yet desired "godly offspring (zera')" — highlighting that monogamous covenant marriage is the appointed context for raising covenant children. THEOLOGICAL: The ruach (spirit/breath, Strong's H7307) of God is seen as the animating principle behind the one-flesh union, connecting creation theology to marriage ethics.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: For I hate divorce (shelach), says YHWH, the God of Israel, and him who covers his garment with violence, says YHWH of hosts; therefore guard yourselves in your spirit (ruach), and do not deal treacherously.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb shelach ("to send away/divorce") is used in the context of legal dismissal of a wife; YHWH's statement "I hate divorce" (Hebrew: ki-sane' shelach) is a direct and unambiguous moral declaration. The MT reads "he hates" (third person) in some manuscripts, creating a textual variant, but the first-person reading ("I hate") is contextually and canonically preferred. THEOLOGICAL: This is the strongest direct statement against divorce in the OT, grounding the prohibition not in social stability alone but in the character of YHWH himself.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: You have wearied YHWH with your words. Yet you say, "In what have we wearied him?" In your saying, "Everyone who does evil is good in the eyes of YHWH, and he delights in them," or "Where is the God of justice?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing accusation — that Israel has morally inverted reality by calling evil good and questioning divine justice — anticipates the Day of YHWH theme developed in chapter 3. TYPOLOGY: The question "Where is the God of justice?" sets up the answer of Mal 3:1, the coming of the messenger and then YHWH himself to his temple, fulfilled in the ministry of John the Baptist and Jesus Christ (Matt 11:10; Mark 1:2).

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: "Behold, I am sending my messenger, and he shall prepare the way before me. And suddenly there shall come to his temple the Lord (ha-adon) whom you are seeking, and the messenger of the covenant in whom you delight — behold, he is coming," says YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "My messenger" (Heb. mal'aki, H4397) echoes the book's title and is explicitly identified as Elijah in 4:5, fulfilled in John the Baptist (Matt 11:10; Mark 1:2; Luke 7:27). "The Lord (ha-adon) whom you are seeking" is a distinct figure from the messenger — the Messiah himself — whose sudden arrival at the temple evokes the Shekinah glory returning (Ezek 43:4). "The messenger of the covenant" carries covenantal overtones linking Sinai to the new covenant inaugurated by Jesus (Heb 8:6-13). HEBREW: The definite article on "adon" (ha-adon) is emphatic, indicating "the Lord" as a specific, expected figure, distinguishable from YHWH's messenger who precedes him.

words.m: preposition + verb niphal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: But who can endure the day of his coming? And who can stand when he appears? For he is like a refiner's fire and like fullers' soap (borit).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical questions "who can endure?" and "who can stand?" echo the Day of YHWH motif found in Joel 2:11 and Amos 5:18-20, signaling that the Messiah's coming brings both salvation and judgment. "Refiner's fire" points to purifying, not merely punishing, judgment — a theme developed in v. 3 (1 Pet 1:7; 1 Cor 3:13-15). HEBREW: "Borit" (H1287) refers to a plant-based alkaline soap used by fullers to cleanse cloth — a vivid image of radical moral purification.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And he shall sit as a refiner and purifier of silver, and he shall purify the sons of Levi and refine them like gold and like silver, and they shall be to YHWH those who bring offerings in righteousness.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The purification of the sons of Levi anticipates the NT priesthood of all believers (1 Pet 2:5, 9; Rev 1:6), wherein Christ as High Priest purifies his people to offer spiritual sacrifices. The image of the refiner who "sits" (Heb. yashav) suggests patient, deliberate, ongoing work — not a momentary act. HISTORICAL: Ancient silver refining required the craftsman to remain seated, watching intently until he could see his reflection in the molten metal — a striking portrait of Christ's sanctifying scrutiny.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Then the offering of Judah and Jerusalem shall be pleasing to YHWH, as in the days of old and as in former years.

words.g: and to stretch or spread out

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: "And I will draw near to you for judgment, and I will be a swift witness against the sorcerers, against the adulterers, against those who swear falsely, and against those who oppress the hired worker in his wages, the widow and the fatherless, and who thrust aside the sojourner, and do not fear me," says YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH lists covenant violations in descending social order — sorcerers, adulterers, false swearers, oppressors of the vulnerable — reflecting the Torah's ethical demands (Exod 22:21-24; Deut 24:14-15). The phrase "do not fear me" (yare') identifies the root cause of all the listed sins: the absence of the fear of YHWH. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's declaration "I will draw near for judgment" positions him as the Divine Judge presiding over covenantal legal proceedings, a courtroom scene common in the prophets (Isa 1:18; Mic 6:1-2).

verses.flow: "For I, YHWH, have not changed; therefore you, O sons of Jacob, have not been consumed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's immutability (Heb. lo shaniti, "I have not changed") is the basis of Israel's preservation despite persistent covenant unfaithfulness. This verse is a foundational proof text for divine immutability (Num 23:19; Jas 1:17). The mercy implied — that Israel's existence depends not on their faithfulness but on YHWH's unchanging nature — is a profound expression of hesed (H2617). HEBREW: "Consumed" (kalah, H3615) carries the sense of being utterly destroyed, finished — the same root used of Israel's near-obliteration in other prophetic warnings.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: From the days of your fathers you have turned aside from my statutes and have not kept them. Return to me, and I will return to you," says YHWH of hosts. "But you say, 'In what shall we return?'"

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
verb qal perfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: "Will a man rob (qaba) God? Yet you are robbing me. But you say, 'How have we robbed you?' In the tithes (ma'aser) and contributions (terumah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rob" (qaba, H6906) is a rare and forceful term, occurring only here and in Prov 22:23. It implies seizing or defrauding by deceit or force — a shocking accusation directed at the covenant people toward their God. "Ma'aser" (H4643) is the tithe, one-tenth of produce and livestock required by Torah (Lev 27:30-32; Num 18:21-29). "Terumah" (H8641) refers to the contribution or heave-offering, a portion lifted up and set apart for priestly use.

verses.flow: With a curse you are cursed, for you are robbing me — the whole nation of you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur + suffix
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Bring the full tithe into the storehouse, that there may be food in my house. And thereby put me to the test, says YHWH of hosts, if I will not open for you the windows of heaven and pour down for you a blessing until there is no more need.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the only verse in the Hebrew Bible where YHWH explicitly invites his people to "test" (bachan, H974) him — normally a prohibited act (Deut 6:16; Matt 4:7). This is not a loophole for presumption but a covenantal promise: faithful obedience to the tithe will be met with overflowing blessing. "Windows of heaven" (arubot hashamayim) echoes the Flood narrative (Gen 7:11) but now inverted — instead of judgment, abundance pours forth. HISTORICAL: The "storehouse" (otzar) was a physical treasury chamber in the Jerusalem temple complex where tithes were deposited (Neh 13:12-13).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I will rebuke the devourer for you, so that it will not destroy the fruits of your soil, and your vine in the field shall not fail to bear," says YHWH of hosts.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: "And all the nations shall call you blessed, for you shall be a land of delight (hephets)," says YHWH of hosts.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: "Your words have been hard (hazaq) against me," says YHWH. "But you say, 'How have we spoken against you?'"
"You have said, 'It is vain (shav') to serve God. And what is the profit that we have kept his charge and that we have walked in mourning before YHWH of hosts?

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: And now we call the arrogant blessed. Evildoers are not only built up but they also test God and escape.'"

words.g: and to prick up the ears
words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then those who feared YHWH spoke with one another. And YHWH listened and heard, and a book of remembrance was written before him of those who feared YHWH and esteemed his name.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "book of remembrance" (sepher zikkaron, H2146) is a heavenly ledger of the faithful, a concept rooted in ancient ANE royal practice where kings maintained rolls of loyal subjects (Esth 6:1; Ps 139:16; Dan 12:1; Rev 20:12). The verb "feared" (yare', H3372) frames covenantal loyalty as reverent awe — the antithesis of the arrogance described in vv. 13-15. This communal conversation of the faithful during apostasy prefigures the remnant theology central to Paul's argument in Rom 11:1-5. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The heavenly record being written "before him" (lephanav) implies a celestial court context, where YHWH presides as King over an assembled council that witnesses and documents the deeds of the faithful.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: "They shall be mine," says YHWH of hosts, "on the day that I am preparing — my treasured possession (segullah). And I will have compassion on them as a man has compassion on his son who serves him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Segullah" (H5459) is a covenant term of intimate personal ownership — used of Israel as YHWH's "treasured possession" in Exod 19:5, Deut 7:6, and 14:2. In ancient Near Eastern usage, segullah referred to a king's private treasury of jewels or prized items set apart from state property. TYPOLOGY: The father-son imagery anticipates the eschatological adoption motif of the NT, wherein believers are confirmed as sons through the Spirit (Rom 8:14-17; Gal 4:5-7), and the "day I am preparing" looks forward to the Parousia (1 Thess 4:17; Matt 25:34).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Then once more you shall see the distinction between the righteous and the wicked, between one who serves God and one who does not serve him."
